chunk_summary: Guidelines for transitioning from stabilization to rehabilitation feeding in children with stunting and severe acute malnutrition, including discharge follow-up criteria to prevent relapse and ensure sustained recovery.
learning_objectives: Understand the staged approach to nutritional rehabilitation after stabilization in children with stunting and severe acute malnutrition.
Learn the criteria for transitioning from starter diets to higher-energy catch-up feeding.
Recognize the importance of follow-up schedules and addressing determinants like food insecurity post-discharge.
Identify high-risk failure patterns such as stalled weight gain or recurring edema.
key_facts: Rehabilitation feeding should gradually increase energy intake up to 220 kcal/kg/day to prevent complications.
Transition to catch-up feeding occurs only when the child is clinically stable, appetite has returned, and edema is reducing.
Follow-up schedule: one week post-discharge, every two weeks in the first month, then monthly until recovery thresholds are met.
Discharge is not the endpoint; relapse and intercurrent infections are common without proper follow-up.
Outpatient clinicians should document discharge phenotype, feeding regimen, and medications, and link families to community nutrition platforms.
reference_quote: Facility-based manuals describe transition from a starter diet to higher-energy catch-up feeding only once the child is clinically stable, appetite has returned, and edema is reducing.
document_key_topics: Prevention of relapse in malnourished children
Addressing determinants like food insecurity and sanitation
